q: How often should a business review its insurance coverage?
a: At minimum, every business should review its insurance program at each annual renewal.
sa: At minimum, every business should review its insurance program at each annual renewal. Any significant business change, such as new employees, new locations, new equipment, or a new service line, should also trigger an immediate mid-year review.

q: When does a business need commercial auto coverage instead of a personal policy?
a: A business needs commercial auto coverage whenever vehicles are titled to a business, used to transport goods or clients for a fee, or regularly used by employees on company business.
sa: A business needs commercial auto coverage whenever vehicles are titled to a business, used to transport goods or clients for a fee, or regularly used by employees on company business. Personal auto policies exclude most business use.

q: What is the difference between a business owners policy and standalone commercial coverage?
a: A business owners policy bundles general liability and commercial property into one policy at a packaged price, making it efficient for qualifying small businesses.
sa: A business owners policy bundles general liability and commercial property into one policy at a packaged price, making it efficient for qualifying small businesses. Standalone commercial coverage provides each line separately, offering more flexibility for complex operations.

q: What types of insurance does a Georgia small business typically need?
a: Most Georgia small businesses need general liability, commercial property, commercial auto (if vehicles are used for business), and workers compensation if they have employees.
sa: Most Georgia small businesses need general liability, commercial property, commercial auto (if vehicles are used for business), and workers compensation if they have employees. Professional services may also need errors and omissions coverage.

q: What should I do if I cannot reach my carrier after hours?
a: Most carriers operate 24-hour claims lines.
sa: Most carriers operate 24-hour claims lines. If you cannot get through, try the carrier's website or mobile app to file online, contact your agent's emergency line if available, or document everything and call first thing in the morning.

q: Is my agent's phone number the same as my carrier's claims number?
a: No.
sa: No. Your agent and your carrier are separate contacts. Your agent can help you navigate a claim, but the claims department is a distinct team at the insurance company, available 24/7, while your agent may only be reachable during business hours.

q: What information do I need when calling my insurer after an accident?
a: Have your policy number, the date, time, and location of the accident, contact and insurance information for all parties
sa: Have your policy number, the date, time, and location of the accident, contact and insurance information for all parties involved, a description of what happened, and photos of the vehicles and scene if safely collected.

q: How do I find the claims phone number for my insurance carrier?
a: Your carrier's claims number appears on your insurance ID card, your declarations page, and the carrier's website.
sa: Your carrier's claims number appears on your insurance ID card, your declarations page, and the carrier's website. It is different from your agent's number and is available 24 hours a day, 7 days a week for most major carriers.

q: Does roadside assistance cover a tow if my car breaks down, not just in an accident?
a: Yes.
sa: Yes. Roadside assistance coverage is specifically designed for non-accident breakdowns, including mechanical failure, dead batteries, flat tires, and running out of fuel. It applies whenever your vehicle is disabled, regardless of whether a collision occurred.

q: What should I do if my car is towed to a shop I did not choose?
a: You have the right to choose where your vehicle is repaired.
sa: You have the right to choose where your vehicle is repaired. If it was towed to a facility you did not select, you can request a second tow to your preferred shop, though you may be responsible for additional tow costs.

q: What is the difference between towing coverage on my policy and roadside assistance towing?
a: Policy towing coverage reimburses a tow following a covered claim.
sa: Policy towing coverage reimburses a tow following a covered claim. Roadside assistance towing is a separate benefit that covers non-accident situations like breakdowns, dead batteries, or lockouts, often without requiring a claim.

q: Does my auto insurance cover towing after an accident?
a: Yes, if you carry comprehensive or collision coverage, your policy typically covers towing to the nearest qualified repair facility after a covered accident.
sa: Yes, if you carry comprehensive or collision coverage, your policy typically covers towing to the nearest qualified repair facility after a covered accident. Coverage limits and conditions vary by carrier and policy.

q: How long does it take to get lockout service?
a: Average response times for roadside lockout service range from 30 to 60 minutes in urban and suburban areas, though rural or remote locations may take longer.
sa: Average response times for roadside lockout service range from 30 to 60 minutes in urban and suburban areas, though rural or remote locations may take longer. Your dispatch center can give you a real-time estimate when you call.

q: Does roadside assistance cover a vehicle lockout?
a: Yes.
sa: Yes. Lockout service is a standard benefit in most roadside assistance programs. If you lock your keys inside your vehicle, a service technician is dispatched to unlock the car using professional tools.

q: What happens if my battery cannot be jump-started on site?
a: If your battery is too depleted or damaged to accept a jump-start, your roadside assistance coverage typically transitio
sa: If your battery is too depleted or damaged to accept a jump-start, your roadside assistance coverage typically transitions to a tow benefit to the nearest qualified repair shop.

q: Does roadside assistance cover a dead car battery?
a: Yes.
sa: Yes. Battery jump-start service is one of the most common benefits included in roadside assistance programs. A service technician is dispatched to your location to restart your vehicle at no additional cost per call.

q: What if my car needs to be towed farther than the policy limit?
a: If your vehicle needs to be towed beyond your policy's mileage limit, your insurer typically covers the tow up to the li
sa: If your vehicle needs to be towed beyond your policy's mileage limit, your insurer typically covers the tow up to the limit and you pay the difference for the remaining distance.

q: What towing distance does roadside assistance typically cover?
a: Most roadside assistance programs cover towing to the nearest qualified repair facility, with mileage limits that common
sa: Most roadside assistance programs cover towing to the nearest qualified repair facility, with mileage limits that commonly range from 10 to 100 miles depending on the plan.

q: How do I use my roadside assistance benefit?
a: To use your roadside assistance benefit, call the number on your insurance card or app, provide your location and a description of the problem, and wait for the dispatched service provider.
sa: To use your roadside assistance benefit, call the number on your insurance card or app, provide your location and a description of the problem, and wait for the dispatched service provider. Have your policy number handy.

q: Is roadside assistance included in my auto policy?
a: Roadside assistance is usually an optional add-on, not a standard feature of a personal auto policy.
sa: Roadside assistance is usually an optional add-on, not a standard feature of a personal auto policy. Some insurers bundle it into higher-tier packages or offer it as an endorsement for a small additional premium.

q: What does roadside assistance cover?
a: Roadside assistance typically covers towing to a nearby repair shop, battery jump-starts, flat tire changes, fuel delivery when you run out of gas, and lockout service.
sa: Roadside assistance typically covers towing to a nearby repair shop, battery jump-starts, flat tire changes, fuel delivery when you run out of gas, and lockout service. Coverage specifics and mileage limits vary by policy.

q: How often should I review my personal insurance coverage?
a: A thorough coverage review once a year, plus a quick check after any major life change, keeps your policies aligned with
sa: A thorough coverage review once a year, plus a quick check after any major life change, keeps your policies aligned with what you actually own and who depends on you.

q: How does bundling home and auto insurance save money?
a: Carriers offer multi-policy discounts when you place both home and auto with the same company, often reducing your total
sa: Carriers offer multi-policy discounts when you place both home and auto with the same company, often reducing your total premium by 5 to 25 percent depending on the carrier and your profile.

q: What types of personal insurance does an independent agent typically offer?
a: An independent agent can shop multiple carriers to find coverage across auto, home, renters, umbrella, and other persona
sa: An independent agent can shop multiple carriers to find coverage across auto, home, renters, umbrella, and other personal lines, all tailored to your specific situation rather than locked to one carrier's products.

q: What happens if the other driver does not have insurance?
a: If the at-fault driver is uninsured, your uninsured motorist coverage steps in to pay for your injuries and, in many sta
sa: If the at-fault driver is uninsured, your uninsured motorist coverage steps in to pay for your injuries and, in many states, your property damage.

q: How does the auto insurance claims process work for collision damage?
a: After you report a collision claim, an adjuster evaluates the damage, a repair estimate is prepared, and your insurer pa
sa: After you report a collision claim, an adjuster evaluates the damage, a repair estimate is prepared, and your insurer pays the covered amount minus your deductible.

q: What should I do at the scene of an accident before calling my insurer?
a: Stay calm, ensure everyone is safe, call 911 if needed, document the scene thoroughly, and exchange information with all
sa: Stay calm, ensure everyone is safe, call 911 if needed, document the scene thoroughly, and exchange information with all parties before contacting your insurer.

q: Does homeowners insurance cover additional living expenses if I cannot stay in my home?
a: Yes, most standard homeowners policies include additional living expense coverage that pays for temporary housing and re
sa: Yes, most standard homeowners policies include additional living expense coverage that pays for temporary housing and reasonable increased costs of living when a covered loss makes your home uninhabitable.

q: How does my homeowners deductible apply to a claim?
a: Your deductible is the amount you pay out of pocket before your insurance coverage applies.
sa: Your deductible is the amount you pay out of pocket before your insurance coverage applies. It is subtracted from your claim payment, and some policies carry separate, higher deductibles for wind and hail events.

q: What does homeowners insurance cover in a typical property damage claim?
a: A standard homeowners policy typically covers sudden and accidental damage to the dwelling structure, attached structures, and personal property from named causes such as fire, wind, hail, theft, and vandalism.
sa: A standard homeowners policy typically covers sudden and accidental damage to the dwelling structure, attached structures, and personal property from named causes such as fire, wind, hail, theft, and vandalism. Flood and earthquake are not included.

q: How do I document my belongings before a loss occurs?
a: Create a home inventory with photos, video walkthroughs, and written descriptions of your belongings.
sa: Create a home inventory with photos, video walkthroughs, and written descriptions of your belongings. Store a copy offsite or in the cloud so it is accessible after a disaster.

q: Should I make temporary repairs before the adjuster visits?
a: Yes, you should make reasonable temporary repairs to prevent further damage, but document everything first and save all receipts.
sa: Yes, you should make reasonable temporary repairs to prevent further damage, but document everything first and save all receipts. Do not make permanent repairs until the adjuster has completed the inspection.

q: What are the most important steps to take immediately after a loss?
a: Contact your insurer promptly, document everything, make only emergency temporary repairs, and keep records of all relat
sa: Contact your insurer promptly, document everything, make only emergency temporary repairs, and keep records of all related expenses from day one.

q: How do I know if my insurance company is acting in bad faith?
a: Warning signs include unreasonable delays without explanation, a denial with no written reason, an offer far below docum
sa: Warning signs include unreasonable delays without explanation, a denial with no written reason, an offer far below documented repair costs, failure to respond to your calls or letters, or pressure to accept a quick settlement before you fully understand your damages.

q: What does O.C.G.A. Section 33-4-6 mean for policyholders?
a: O.C.G.A.
sa: O.C.G.A. Section 33-4-6 gives Georgia policyholders the right to sue for the claim amount plus a financial penalty and attorney fees if their insurer refused to pay a covered claim without reasonable cause.

q: What is insurance bad faith in Georgia?
a: Insurance bad faith in Georgia occurs when an insurer refuses to pay a legitimate claim, delays payment unreasonably, or
sa: Insurance bad faith in Georgia occurs when an insurer refuses to pay a legitimate claim, delays payment unreasonably, or engages in deceptive or unfair claims practices in violation of Georgia law.

q: What is the difference between disputing a claim and filing a complaint with the Georgia Department of Insurance?
a: Disputing a claim is a direct negotiation with your insurer over amount or coverage.
sa: Disputing a claim is a direct negotiation with your insurer over amount or coverage. Filing a DOI complaint is a regulatory action that asks the state to review whether your insurer followed Georgia's claims handling rules.

q: What is the insurance appraisal process and when does it apply?
a: The appraisal process is a policy-based mechanism for resolving disputes about the dollar value of a covered loss.
sa: The appraisal process is a policy-based mechanism for resolving disputes about the dollar value of a covered loss. Each side hires its own appraiser, and a neutral umpire breaks any tie.

q: How do I dispute an insurance claim settlement I disagree with?
a: Start by requesting a written explanation of the settlement, then escalate through your insurer's formal appeal process,
sa: Start by requesting a written explanation of the settlement, then escalate through your insurer's formal appeal process, invoke the appraisal clause if available, or file a complaint with the Georgia Department of Insurance.

q: Can a denied insurance claim be overturned?
a: Yes.
sa: Yes. Denials are overturned regularly through internal carrier appeals, state insurance department complaints, independent appraisal processes, and litigation. The most effective path depends on the denial reason.

q: What should I do immediately after my insurance claim is denied?
a: Read the denial letter carefully to identify the specific reason, gather your policy documents, and submit a written app
sa: Read the denial letter carefully to identify the specific reason, gather your policy documents, and submit a written appeal or request for reconsideration within the timeframe stated in the letter.

q: What are the most common reasons an insurance claim gets denied?
a: The most common denial reasons are: the cause of loss is excluded by the policy, the damage falls below the deductible,
sa: The most common denial reasons are: the cause of loss is excluded by the policy, the damage falls below the deductible, the policy lapsed for non-payment, or required documentation was not provided on time.

q: How long does it take to get paid after an insurance claim is approved in Georgia?
a: Once you and your insurer reach a written agreement on a settlement, Georgia regulations require the insurer to issue pa
sa: Once you and your insurer reach a written agreement on a settlement, Georgia regulations require the insurer to issue payment within 10 business days.

q: What happens if my Georgia insurer misses the 60-day decision deadline?
a: Missing the 60-day deadline can constitute an unfair claims practice under Georgia law, and if the delay is unreasonable
sa: Missing the 60-day deadline can constitute an unfair claims practice under Georgia law, and if the delay is unreasonable and without cause, it may expose the insurer to bad faith penalties including a 25 percent surcharge on the covered loss plus attorney fees.

q: How long does my insurance carrier have to respond to my claim in Georgia?
a: Georgia regulations require your insurer to acknowledge your claim within 15 calendar days, and to approve or deny it wi
sa: Georgia regulations require your insurer to acknowledge your claim within 15 calendar days, and to approve or deny it within 60 calendar days after you submit all required documentation.

q: Can I get multiple repair estimates after a property insurance claim?
a: Yes, you can and often should get more than one repair estimate after a property insurance claim.
sa: Yes, you can and often should get more than one repair estimate after a property insurance claim. Multiple bids help verify that the adjuster's settlement figure reflects actual market costs, and your estimates can be shared directly with the adjuster to support a fair settlement.

q: What does an insurance adjuster do, and how do I work with one?
a: An insurance adjuster inspects your loss, reviews your policy coverage, and determines the settlement amount the insurer will offer.
sa: An insurance adjuster inspects your loss, reviews your policy coverage, and determines the settlement amount the insurer will offer. Prepare by documenting everything and bringing your estimates. You can disagree with an adjuster's findings and request reconsideration.

q: What is the insurance claims process step by step?
a: The claims process begins with notifying your insurer, then documenting the loss, submitting a claim form, cooperating with an adjuster's inspection, reviewing the settlement offer, and accepting payment.
sa: The claims process begins with notifying your insurer, then documenting the loss, submitting a claim form, cooperating with an adjuster's inspection, reviewing the settlement offer, and accepting payment. Each step has timing expectations, and keeping records throughout protects your interests.

q: What happens if I wait too long to file an insurance claim?
a: Waiting too long to file a claim can result in denial based on a policy's prompt-reporting requirement.
sa: Waiting too long to file a claim can result in denial based on a policy's prompt-reporting requirement. Evidence degrades, witnesses become unavailable, and documentation gaps give insurers grounds to question the loss. Most policies expect notification as soon as reasonably possible.

q: What information do I need to report when filing a homeowners or auto claim?
a: When filing a claim, have your policy number, contact details, a description of what happened, documentation of the damage, and, for auto incidents, the other party's insurance and vehicle information.
sa: When filing a claim, have your policy number, contact details, a description of what happened, documentation of the damage, and, for auto incidents, the other party's insurance and vehicle information. Complete, accurate reporting from the start speeds up the entire process.

q: When should I file an insurance claim vs. handle it out of pocket?
a: File a claim when the loss clearly exceeds your deductible by a meaningful margin and represents a sudden, significant event.
sa: File a claim when the loss clearly exceeds your deductible by a meaningful margin and represents a sudden, significant event. For smaller losses close to your deductible, paying out of pocket often protects your premium and renewal standing.

q: How do I file an NFIP flood claim?
a: Report through your Write-Your-Own carrier (Selective) using your policy contact; document with photos; Olive Cover can
sa: Report through your Write-Your-Own carrier (Selective) using your policy contact; document with photos; Olive Cover can help you manage it.

q: Do I need flood insurance in Georgia?
a: Flood is excluded from standard policies and needs its own coverage.
sa: Flood is excluded from standard policies and needs its own coverage. Required in FEMA high-risk zones and worth considering elsewhere too.

q: What does NFIP flood insurance cover?
a: Building and contents against flood: up to $250,000 building / $100,000 contents for homes, $500,000 each for commercial
sa: Building and contents against flood: up to $250,000 building / $100,000 contents for homes, $500,000 each for commercial; private excess can layer on top.

q: Does Olive Cover offer NFIP flood insurance?
a: Yes, Olive Cover places NFIP flood coverage for Georgia homes and businesses (through Selective as a Write-Your-Own carr
sa: Yes, Olive Cover places NFIP flood coverage for Georgia homes and businesses (through Selective as a Write-Your-Own carrier).

q: How do I file a Hanover claim in Georgia?
a: Call 1-800-628-0250 (24/7) or use the Hanover claims portal; Olive Cover can help you manage it.
sa: Call 1-800-628-0250 (24/7) or use the Hanover claims portal; Olive Cover can help you manage it.

q: What is Hanover known for in personal lines?
a: Account-based personal lines with strong bundled home-and-auto value, in the standard-to-preferred market.
sa: Account-based personal lines with strong bundled home-and-auto value, in the standard-to-preferred market.

q: Does Olive Cover offer Hanover home and auto insurance?
a: Yes, Olive Cover places Hanover home, auto, condo, renters, and umbrella for Georgia households.
sa: Yes, Olive Cover places Hanover home, auto, condo, renters, and umbrella for Georgia households.

q: How do I file a Chubb commercial claim in Georgia?
a: Call 1-800-252-4670 (24/7) or use the Chubb claims portal; Olive Cover can help you manage it.
sa: Call 1-800-252-4670 (24/7) or use the Chubb claims portal; Olive Cover can help you manage it.

q: Is Chubb a good fit for my business?
a: Best for mid-to-large or complex businesses (roughly $5M+ revenue); usually not the cheapest market for very small busin
sa: Best for mid-to-large or complex businesses (roughly $5M+ revenue); usually not the cheapest market for very small businesses.

q: What does Chubb Commercial specialize in?
a: Mid-market and specialty commercial: management liability, professional liability, cyber, and complex property and packa
sa: Mid-market and specialty commercial: management liability, professional liability, cyber, and complex property and package coverage.

q: Does Olive Cover offer Chubb commercial insurance?
a: Yes, Olive Cover places Chubb specialty and mid-market commercial coverage for eligible Georgia businesses.
sa: Yes, Olive Cover places Chubb specialty and mid-market commercial coverage for eligible Georgia businesses.

q: Is Hanover a good fit for my home and auto?
a: Good value for newer homes and standard drivers, especially bundled home and auto; higher-value or non-standard homes ma
sa: Good value for newer homes and standard drivers, especially bundled home and auto; higher-value or non-standard homes may fit another carrier better.

q: Is Hanover available in Georgia, and how do I file a claim?
a: Yes, Hanover is admitted in Georgia for commercial lines.
sa: Yes, Hanover is admitted in Georgia for commercial lines. File a claim 24/7 at 1-800-628-0250 or through the Hanover claims portal.

q: Is Hanover a good fit for my business?
a: Best for standard, preferred small and mid-size businesses (roughly $500,000 to $25M revenue); not the market for large
sa: Best for standard, preferred small and mid-size businesses (roughly $500,000 to $25M revenue); not the market for large or specialty risks.

q: What is Hanover known for?
a: An account-based approach to small and mid-size commercial insurance, with industry-tailored programs across hundreds of
sa: An account-based approach to small and mid-size commercial insurance, with industry-tailored programs across hundreds of business classes.

q: Does Olive Cover offer Hanover insurance?
a: Yes, for both personal (home, auto, umbrella) and business insurance.
sa: Yes, for both personal (home, auto, umbrella) and business insurance.

q: Is Chubb available in Georgia, and how do I file a claim?
a: Yes, Chubb is admitted in Georgia.
sa: Yes, Chubb is admitted in Georgia. File a claim 24/7 at 1-800-252-4670 or through the Chubb claims portal.

q: Is Chubb a good fit for my home?
a: Best for higher-value homes, valuable collections, or higher umbrella limits; usually not the lowest-cost option for sta
sa: Best for higher-value homes, valuable collections, or higher umbrella limits; usually not the lowest-cost option for standard homes.

q: What is Chubb known for?
a: Higher-value home and personal coverage (Masterpiece) plus specialty commercial lines, with broad coverage and strong cl
sa: Higher-value home and personal coverage (Masterpiece) plus specialty commercial lines, with broad coverage and strong claims service.

q: Does Olive Cover offer Chubb insurance?
a: Yes.
sa: Yes. Olive Cover is a directly appointed Chubb agency for personal and specialty commercial lines.

q: Can I get pet insurance for older animals?
a: Yes, but premiums are higher and pre-existing conditions are excluded.
sa: Yes, but premiums are higher and pre-existing conditions are excluded. Enrolling earlier is significantly cheaper over the pet's lifetime.

q: Is pet insurance worth the cost?
a: It depends on the pet, but a single major surgery can exceed five years of premiums.
sa: It depends on the pet, but a single major surgery can exceed five years of premiums.

q: What does pet insurance cover?
a: Accidents, illnesses, surgery, hospitalization, prescriptions, and (on richer plans) wellness exams and dental.
sa: Accidents, illnesses, surgery, hospitalization, prescriptions, and (on richer plans) wellness exams and dental.

q: Does homeowners insurance cover pet bites?
a: Standard homeowners liability covers most dog bites, but several breeds are excluded by major carriers.
sa: Standard homeowners liability covers most dog bites, but several breeds are excluded by major carriers.

q: What is pet insurance?
a: Pet insurance is medical insurance for cats and dogs that reimburses veterinary expenses after a deductible.
sa: Pet insurance is medical insurance for cats and dogs that reimburses veterinary expenses after a deductible.

q: What is equine liability insurance?
a: Coverage for bodily injury or property damage caused by your horse, including riding lessons, boarding, and showing.
sa: Coverage for bodily injury or property damage caused by your horse, including riding lessons, boarding, and showing.

q: How much does equine insurance cost?
a: Mortality typically runs 3.5 to 5 percent of the horse's value per year; major medical is $200 to $700 in addition.
sa: Mortality typically runs 3.5 to 5 percent of the horse's value per year; major medical is $200 to $700 in addition.

q: What types of equine insurance are available?
a: Mortality, major medical, surgical, loss-of-use, and equine liability (personal or commercial).
sa: Mortality, major medical, surgical, loss-of-use, and equine liability (personal or commercial).

q: Does homeowners insurance cover horses?
a: Standard homeowners covers horse-related personal liability only for non-commercial pets, with low limits.
sa: Standard homeowners covers horse-related personal liability only for non-commercial pets, with low limits. Boarding, training, or showing requires equine-specific coverage.

q: What is equine insurance?
a: Equine insurance covers horses against death, theft, major medical expenses, and liability exposures.
sa: Equine insurance covers horses against death, theft, major medical expenses, and liability exposures.

q: How much commercial crime coverage do I need?
a: Most small businesses start at $100,000 to $250,000; higher for businesses with payroll over $1 million or client trust
sa: Most small businesses start at $100,000 to $250,000; higher for businesses with payroll over $1 million or client trust funds.

q: What is the difference between fidelity bonds and crime insurance?
a: Fidelity bonds are a narrower form of crime coverage focused on employee theft, often required by clients or contracts.
sa: Fidelity bonds are a narrower form of crime coverage focused on employee theft, often required by clients or contracts.

q: What does commercial crime insurance cover?
a: Employee theft, forgery, computer fraud, funds transfer fraud, robbery, and counterfeit currency.
sa: Employee theft, forgery, computer fraud, funds transfer fraud, robbery, and counterfeit currency.

q: Who needs commercial crime insurance?
a: Any business that handles cash, payroll, customer funds, or has employees with access to financial systems.
sa: Any business that handles cash, payroll, customer funds, or has employees with access to financial systems.

q: What is commercial crime insurance?
a: Commercial crime insurance protects a business against employee theft, forgery, computer fraud, and other dishonest acts
sa: Commercial crime insurance protects a business against employee theft, forgery, computer fraud, and other dishonest acts.

q: Can I add identity theft coverage to my homeowners policy?
a: Yes, most homeowners and renters carriers offer it as an endorsement for $25 to $60 a year.
sa: Yes, most homeowners and renters carriers offer it as an endorsement for $25 to $60 a year.

q: How is identity theft insurance different from credit monitoring?
a: Credit monitoring alerts you when fraud happens; identity theft insurance pays the recovery cost.
sa: Credit monitoring alerts you when fraud happens; identity theft insurance pays the recovery cost.

q: Is identity theft insurance worth it?
a: It is inexpensive (often $25 to $60 a year as a homeowners add-on) and saves significant recovery time after a major fra
sa: It is inexpensive (often $25 to $60 a year as a homeowners add-on) and saves significant recovery time after a major fraud event.

q: What does identity theft insurance cover?
a: Recovery expenses: legal fees, lost wages, document replacement, and credit-monitoring services.
sa: Recovery expenses: legal fees, lost wages, document replacement, and credit-monitoring services.

q: What is identity theft insurance?
a: Identity theft insurance helps cover the out-of-pocket costs of restoring your identity after fraud.
sa: Identity theft insurance helps cover the out-of-pocket costs of restoring your identity after fraud.

q: How much does equipment breakdown coverage cost?
a: Most businesses pay $200 to $1,500 annually when added as an endorsement to business owners policy or commercial property.
sa: Most businesses pay $200 to $1,500 annually when added as an endorsement to business owners policy or commercial property. Standalone equipment breakdown for larger operations runs higher, scaling with equipment values and spoilage exposure.

q: Does equipment breakdown cover spoilage of refrigerated goods?
a: Yes, when the spoilage is caused by a covered equipment breakdown event.
sa: Yes, when the spoilage is caused by a covered equipment breakdown event. Critical coverage for restaurants, grocery stores, food service, and pharmaceutical businesses.

q: What's the difference between equipment breakdown and commercial property?
a: Commercial property covers external perils: fire, theft, weather, vandalism.
sa: Commercial property covers external perils: fire, theft, weather, vandalism. Equipment breakdown covers internal failures: boiler explosion, electrical arc, motor burnout, refrigeration compressor failure. Both are needed.

q: When does builders risk coverage end?
a: At the earlier of substantial completion, occupancy, or a fixed end date in the policy.
sa: At the earlier of substantial completion, occupancy, or a fixed end date in the policy. After that, standard homeowners or commercial property must take over.

q: Who is responsible for builders risk: owner or contractor?
a: Depends on the contract.
sa: Depends on the contract. Most residential construction puts the owner in charge of builders risk (it's their building). Commercial projects often have the contractor carry it. Always written into the construction contract.

q: Is builders risk required for Georgia construction projects?
a: Most construction loans require it.
sa: Most construction loans require it. Lenders typically demand builders risk as a condition of the loan because the building under construction is collateral. Cash-funded projects can skip it but should still consider it.

q: What's a bailee's customer policy?
a: Bailee's customer covers property owned by others while it is in the bailee's care, custody, or control.
sa: Bailee's customer covers property owned by others while it is in the bailee's care, custody, or control. Critical for repair shops, dry cleaners, warehouses, and storage facilities.

q: Do contractors need inland marine insurance if they have general liability?
a: Yes for most contractors.
sa: Yes for most contractors. General liability does not cover damage to the contractor's own tools and equipment. Inland marine fills that gap, covering loss of contractor-owned equipment at job sites, in transit, and stored at the shop.

q: What does inland marine insurance cover, despite the name?
a: Inland marine covers property in transit, contractor equipment, mobile machinery, goods held for others, and high-value items that move between locations.
sa: Inland marine covers property in transit, contractor equipment, mobile machinery, goods held for others, and high-value items that move between locations. It has nothing to do with boats or maritime insurance.

q: What does dram shop liability mean in Georgia?
a: Dram shop liability means an establishment can be held legally responsible for damages caused by patrons it served while visibly intoxicated, especially when the patron is a minor.
sa: Dram shop liability means an establishment can be held legally responsible for damages caused by patrons it served while visibly intoxicated, especially when the patron is a minor. Georgia is a dram shop state; liquor liability insurance is the primary defense.

q: How much does liquor liability cost in Georgia?
a: Georgia restaurants typically pay $750 to $5,000 annually.
sa: Georgia restaurants typically pay $750 to $5,000 annually. Bars, nightclubs, and high-alcohol-volume establishments pay more. Pricing scales with alcohol sales, hours of operation, and establishment type.

q: Is liquor liability insurance required in Georgia?
a: Not legally required by state statute for most establishments, but practically required: standard general liability excludes alcohol-related claims, and many landlords, partners, and lenders require it.
sa: Not legally required by state statute for most establishments, but practically required: standard general liability excludes alcohol-related claims, and many landlords, partners, and lenders require it. Liquor licenses may also require proof of coverage.

q: What does ordinance or law coverage do?
a: Ordinance or law pays the additional cost to bring a damaged building up to current code during repair.
sa: Ordinance or law pays the additional cost to bring a damaged building up to current code during repair. Critical for older buildings where code has changed since original construction.

q: What is business interruption insurance?
a: Business interruption (also called business income) replaces lost net profit and continuing expenses when a covered loss shuts down operations.
